title: School Choice, Deferred Acceptance, and Student Outcomes: Evidence from a Centralized Admissions System

sections.title: High School Graduation and College Enrollment
sections.label: sec:grad-college-20

qa.question: What fraction of students receive free or reduced lunch?
Are DA-assigned and non-DA-assigned students different in 4th grade math scores?
qa.answer: Slightly: 0.04 vs -0.04 standard deviations, difference of 0.08, but not statistically significant

qa.question: What fraction of students are English language learners?

qa.question: Are oversubscribed schools higher or lower in average test scores?
qa.answer: Higher: 0.41 vs -0.24 standard deviations

qa.question: Do oversubscribed schools have fewer free/reduced lunch students?
What is the average school enrollment?
qa.answer: 482 students

tables.caption: Welfare Comparison of School Assignment Mechanisms
tables.label: tab:welfare-comparison-mechanisms

qa.question: Which mechanism is both stable and strategy-proof?
qa.answer: Student-proposing DA

qa.question: Which mechanism achieves the highest average welfare?
qa.answer: Top Trading Cycles (TTC) with 0.761

qa.question: Is the Boston mechanism stable?
qa.answer: No

qa.question: What fraction of students list only one school?
Does listing more schools increase the chance of assignment to some listed school?
qa.answer: Yes: from 84.1% (1 school) to 95.2% (11+ schools)

qa.question: What is the maximum list length allowed?
qa.answer: 12 schools

qa.question: What fraction of students are assigned to their first choice in 2017-2018?
What is the justified envy rate under DA?
qa.answer: 0.0% -- DA produces stable matchings which eliminate justified envy

qa.question: Has the fraction assigned to first choice been increasing over time?

tables.caption: RDD First Stage: Probability of Attending School at Admission Cutoff
tables.label: tab:rdd-cutoff-effects

qa.question: What is the first-stage jump at the cutoff?
qa.answer: 0.61 -- students just above the cutoff are 61 percentage points more likely to attend the school

qa.question: What is the mean attendance rate just below the cutoff?
What bandwidth is used in the baseline specification?
qa.answer: 10 points (Calonico-Cattaneo-Titiunik MSE-optimal)

tables.caption: RDD Estimates: Effect of Attending Oversubscribed School on 8th Grade Test Scores
tables.label: tab:rdd-test-score-outcomes

qa.question: What is the IV estimate of attending an oversubscribed school on math scores?
qa.answer: 0.30 standard deviations in the baseline specification

qa.question: Is the effect larger for math or reading?
qa.answer: Math (0.30 sd) is larger than reading (0.20 sd)

qa.question: What is the first-stage F-statistic?
qa.answer: 841, far above conventional weak-instrument thresholds

tables.caption: RDD Estimates: Effect of Attending Oversubscribed School on High School Outcomes
tables.label: tab:rdd-graduation

qa.question: What is the IV estimate of attending an oversubscribed school on graduation rate?
qa.answer: 6.9 percentage points in the baseline

qa.question: What is the baseline graduation rate for students just below the cutoff?
Does attending the oversubscribed school increase college enrollment?
qa.answer: Yes: 5.1 percentage point increase in 4-year college enrollment (IV estimate)

tables.caption: Heterogeneous Effects of Attending Oversubscribed School by Student Subgroup
tables.label: tab:subgroup-effects

qa.question: Which subgroup benefits most in math from attending an oversubscribed school?
qa.answer: English language learners (0.41 sd)

qa.question: Are effects larger for free/reduced lunch students than non-FRL students?
qa.answer: Yes, 0.34 vs 0.24 sd for math

tables.caption: Boston Mechanism vs. Deferred Acceptance: Outcomes Under Misreported Preferences
tables.label: tab:boston-vs-da

qa.question: Does manipulation under the Boston mechanism reduce welfare?
qa.answer: Yes, by 6.7 percentage points in Nash equilibrium relative to truthful play

qa.question: Does manipulation under DA reduce welfare?
qa.answer: No, welfare loss is 0.0 pp at all manipulation levels -- DA is strategy-proof

qa.question: Which mechanism assigns more students to their true first choice under truthful play?
qa.answer: Boston mechanism (71.4% vs 66.2% for DA)

tables.caption: TTC vs. DA: Assignment Differences and Welfare
tables.label: tab:ttc-vs-da

qa.question: Does TTC achieve higher average welfare than DA?
Is TTC stable?
qa.answer: No, TTC has 1.84% of student-school pairs with justified envy

qa.question: What fraction of DA matchings are stable?

tables.caption: Appendix: Estimated Preference Parameters from Structural Demand Model
tables.label: tab:appendix-preference-elicitation

gold_cells.variable: Peer free lunch share (10 pp increase)
Peer free lunch share (10 pp increase)

Peer free lunch share (10 pp increase)

qa.question: What is the most important positive factor in school preferences?
qa.answer: School test score (coefficient 1.84 per sd)

qa.question: Does distance deter school choice?
qa.answer: Yes, coefficient -0.42 per km is negative and significant

equations.label: eq:da-step
equations.latex: \text{Step } t: \text{ each unmatched student } i \text{ proposes to best unrejected school } s; \text{ school } s \text{ tentatively holds top-}q_s \text{ and rejects others}
equations.description: Deferred Acceptance algorithm: student proposes, school tentatively holds

qa.question: What happens in each step of the DA algorithm?
qa.answer: Each unmatched student proposes to their most-preferred unrejected school; each school tentatively holds its top-q_s applicants by priority and rejects the rest

equations.label: eq:stability
equations.latex: (i, s) \text{ is a blocking pair if } s \succ_i \mu(i) \text{ and } i \succ_s j \text{ for some } j \in \mu(s), \quad |\mathcal{S}| \leq \binom{n}{2} \cdot \lfloor K/2 \rfloor
equations.description: Stability: no student-school pair prefers each other to their current match

qa.question: What is a blocking pair?
qa.answer: A student i and school s such that s is preferred by i over their current match mu(i), and i is preferred by s over some currently matched student j

equations.label: eq:strategy-proof
equations.description: Strategy-proofness: truthful preference revelation is a dominant strategy under DA

qa.question: What does strategy-proofness mean?
qa.answer: A student cannot improve their assignment by misreporting their preferences: mu_D(true) is weakly preferred to mu_D(misreport) for all misreports and all others' reports

equations.label: eq:justified-envy
equations.latex: (i, s) \text{ has justified envy if } s \succ_i \mu(i) \text{ and } \text{priority}_{is} > \text{priority}_{js} \text{ for some } j \in \mu(s)
equations.description: Justified envy: student i has higher priority than an assigned student at a school s they prefer

qa.question: What is justified envy?
qa.answer: Student i has justified envy if they prefer school s to their current assignment AND have higher priority than some student who was assigned to s

equations.label: eq:boston-manip
equations.description: Boston mechanism is manipulable: misreporting can strictly improve a student's outcome

qa.question: Is the Boston mechanism strategy-proof?
qa.answer: No -- there exist preference reports succ_i' such that the student strictly prefers their assignment under misreporting to truthful play

equations.label: eq:welfare-util
equations.latex: W_U(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^n u_i(\mu(i)), \quad u_i(s) = \beta_1 q_s + \beta_2 d_{is} + \varepsilon_{is}, \quad \#\{\text{matchings}\} = \binom{n + K - 1}{K} \cdot \prod_{s=1}^{K} \lfloor q_s \rfloor !
equations.description: Utilitarian social welfare: sum of student utilities under matching mu

qa.question: How is utilitarian welfare calculated?
qa.answer: Sum of individual utilities u_i(mu(i)) where utility is a function of school quality q_s and distance d_is

equations.label: eq:welfare-rawls
equations.description: Rawlsian social welfare: minimum utility across all students

qa.question: What does Rawlsian welfare capture?
qa.answer: The welfare of the worst-off student -- the minimum utility across all students in the matching

equations.label: eq:match-prob
equations.latex: P_{is} = \Pr(\mu(i) = s) = \Pr\!\left(\text{priority}_{is} \geq \text{priority}_{\lceil q_s \rceil\text{-th}}\right), \quad \begin{bmatrix} P_{i1} \\ P_{i2} \\ \vdots \\ P_{iK} \end{bmatrix} \in [0,1]^K, \; \sum_{s=1}^K P_{is} \leq 1
equations.description: Matching probability: likelihood student i is matched to school s under DA

qa.question: What determines a student's probability of being matched to a school?
qa.answer: Whether the student's priority score at the school is above the cutoff (q_s-th highest applicant priority)

equations.label: eq:rdd
equations.latex: Y_i = \alpha + \tau D_i + f(R_i - c) + \varepsilon_i, \quad D_i = \mathbf{1}[R_i \geq c]
equations.description: RDD at school admission cutoff c; D_i = 1 if student admitted above cutoff

qa.question: What is the identifying variation in the RDD?
qa.answer: Whether student priority score R_i is above or below the school's admission cutoff c; tau is the local average treatment effect for marginal applicants

qa.question: What mechanisms are compared in this paper?
qa.answer: Boston (Immediate Acceptance), student-proposing DA, and Top Trading Cycles (TTC)

qa.question: What is the IV estimate of attending an oversubscribed school on math scores?
qa.answer: 0.30 standard deviations

qa.question: Is student-proposing DA strategy-proof?
qa.answer: Yes -- truthful preference reporting is a dominant strategy under DA

qa.question: Which mechanism achieves higher average welfare, TTC or DA?
qa.answer: TTC achieves higher average welfare (0.761 vs 0.742), but TTC is not stable

qa.question: What is the graduation rate for students just below the admission cutoff?
